text.title: Agency Case Study Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every freelancer/micro-agency case study must satisfy. These are hard rules — operator input and client requests do not override them.

rule.statement: Every case study has exactly five named sections in this order: Context, Problem, Approach, Outcome, Lessons. No section is omitted, none is merged. Total length 600-1200 words.

rationale.source: Bencivenga / Schwartz long-copy case structure; Basecamp customer-story template

rule.rationale: Long-form social proof needs predictable shape so a skim-reader (most portfolio visitors) can land on Outcome in under 10 seconds. The five-section frame matches how prospects mentally evaluate a vendor: "Is this me?" (Context), "What was hurting?" (Problem), "What did you actually do?" (Approach), "Did it work?" (Outcome), "Would you do it again?" (Lessons).

rule.statement: Outcome section must lead with at least one quantified metric paired with its baseline. Allowed formats: absolute delta ("conversion 1.2% → 3.4%"), percentage delta ("+183% MQLs in 12 weeks"), or time saved ("4 hours/week → 0, recovered $620/mo at agency rate"). Never publish "significant improvement" without numbers.

rationale.source: HubSpot 2024 buyer-content study; agency proof conversion benchmarks

rule.rationale: Unquantified outcomes read as marketing copy and fail to anchor pricing conversations. Pairing the metric with its baseline blocks the "from zero to 100%" framing that prospects discount as cherry-picked.

example.title: Quantified outcome with baseline
example.code: Outcome: cart abandonment dropped from 71% (baseline, Q1 2025) to 48% over 8 weeks, recovering ~$14,200/month in net revenue at the client's current AOV of $87. Source: Shopify checkout analytics, week 9 export.
example.note: Both delta and baseline are present; data source is named. The prospect can replicate the framing for their own funnel.

example.title: Vague outcome
example.code: Outcome: significantly improved conversion and recovered substantial revenue. Client was thrilled with the results.
example.note: No numbers, no baseline, no source. Reads as testimonial filler. Prospects discount this entirely.

rule.statement: Include exactly one verbatim client quote in the Outcome section, attributed to a named person (first name + role minimum) with date or engagement-end month. Quote must be verbatim from a written or recorded source — never paraphrased or LLM-tightened. Length 1-3 sentences.

rationale.source: Nielsen Norman Group testimonial trust study (2023); FTC endorsement guides

rule.rationale: Verbatim quotes with named attribution are the cheapest trust signal a freelancer has. Paraphrased quotes ("the client said it was great") are legally risky under FTC §255 endorsement rules and read as fabricated to experienced buyers.

rule.statement: Explicitly state your scope of engagement vs the client's broader team contribution. Use the Approach section header "What I/we did" and call out at least one thing the client team did that you did not, if applicable. Never imply solo authorship of a multi-vendor outcome.

rationale.source: Brennan Dunn proposal-honesty practice; common cause of agency-prospect mistrust

rule.rationale: Prospects vet case studies by comparing them to what their team would need to do. Overclaiming sole credit is the single most common reason a prospect quietly disqualifies a freelancer on the way to the sales call. Honest scoping converts higher because it lets prospects map the case study onto their own constraints.

example.title: Honest scope
example.code: What I did: rebuilt the Shopify checkout funnel (5 screens), wired post-purchase upsell via Bold, set up Klaviyo abandoned-cart flows. What the client team did: copywriting, photography, customer support for the launch week.

rule.statement: One case study covers exactly one engagement with one client. Multi-client roll-ups, retainer-spanning "things I did over a year" lists, and aggregated "across 15 projects we delivered X" are NOT case studies and must be written as separate methodology outputs (portfolio overview, retainer narrative, capabilities sheet).

rationale.source: case-study craft tradition; SEO entity-anchoring requirement

rule.rationale: A single-engagement case is the only artifact that survives skeptical scrutiny: dates, names, numbers all anchor to one verifiable engagement. Multi-engagement summaries cannot be fact-checked by prospects and read as marketing-speak. They also fail to attract long-tail search ("Shopify checkout rebuild Klaviyo case study") that pulls real qualified buyers.
